title: A granular Büttiker-Landauer motor
authors: Olivier Devauchelle, Predrag Popović, Piotr Szymczak, Anaïs Abramian, Arnaud Lazarus
abstract: Random walkers usually diffuse according to Fick's law. On average, they move down the gradient of their concentration and, in the absence of external force, tend to distribute themselves uniformly. In some experiments, however, this familiar notion is at odds with observation. Sand grains, for instance, gather along the nodal lines of a vibrated elastic plate to form a Chladni figure, thus accumulating where fluctuations are weak -- a fact that escapes the reach of Fick's law. On theoretical grounds, Büttiker [Zeitschrift für Physik B, 68, 1987] and Landauer [J Stat Phys, 53, 1988] proposed that particles submitted to a non-uniform temperature field would indeed gather where the temperature is low. They also predicted that, in the presence of a potential force, a non-uniform temperature could drive a steady current of particles, powered only by noise. Here, we present an experimental realization of these phenomena in a macroscopic system, which confirms the quantitative predictions of Büttiker.
summary: The paper experimentally demonstrates a 'granular Büttiker-Landauer motor' using bouncing sand grains. It shows that particle accumulation deviates from classical diffusion laws when diffusivity varies spatially. By applying both a temperature gradient and an external force, the system generates a measurable, non-equilibrium steady current powered solely by noise.
main_problem: The research addresses discrepancies between classical diffusion theory (Fick's law) and experimental observations, aiming to realize a 'granular Büttiker-Landauer motor.' It seeks to show how non-uniform energy landscapes can drive particle currents powered only by noise.
main_result: The experiment confirms that grain density accumulates inversely proportional to local diffusivity ($ho \propto 1/D$), and the system generates a measurable steady current when both a temperature gradient and an external force are applied.
method: The study combines macroscopic experiments using vibrating granular sand on patterned substrates with theoretical analysis based on modified flux equations derived from non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.
model_or_system: The physical platform is quartz sand grains bouncing on a disk, where the substrate diffusivity ($D$) can be varied by embedding soft elastomer pads. The underlying physics models particle transport in non-uniform temperature fields, governed by generalized Smoluchowski or Fokker-Planck type equations.
key_observables: Grain density ($ho$), diffusion coefficients ($D_{	ext{hard}}, D_{	ext{soft}}$), and the resulting steady-state particle current ($j$) or angular momentum ($M$).
important_parameters: The ratio of diffusivities ($D_{	ext{soft}}/D_{	ext{hard}}$) and the dimensionless parameter $ eta_0 f R$ (related to temperature gradient and external force).
main_assumptions:
Key assumptions include applying an alternative flux equation ($\mathbf{j} = -
abla(Dho)$) and assuming that particle interactions are negligible during the hopping process.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup, show steady-state density maps (Fig. 2a), plot variance vs. time to extract $D$ (Fig. 2b), and demonstrate current flow streamlines in simulations under external force (Fig. 3).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the failure of Fick's law using simple density accumulation experiments, to developing a more complex model incorporating external forces and temperature gradients, culminating in measurements of directed particle currents.
why_interesting: This work provides an accessible, macroscopic realization of fundamental non-equilibrium thermodynamic principles (like those governing Brownian motors), which are crucial concepts for understanding energy transduction far from equilibrium, relevant to open quantum systems dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a: Experimental setup. The disk vibrates along the z-axis. The setup can be tilted by a few degrees around the x-axis. Quartz grains bounce randomly over a hard plastic surface (green, PVC), or a soft silicon elastomer (pink, vinyl polysiloxane). b: Still frame from an experimental movie (run C). Dashed white lines show the grains’ trajectories during 1.5 s (75 frames).

FIG. 2. Steady state in a horizontal corral. a: Surface density of grains for run C. The tilt of the setup is less than 0.5◦. b: Variance of the grains’ trajectories along y, as a function of time (symbols). Dotted lines show linear fits. Dashed line indicates the width of the soft pad. c: Ratio of density over the hard disk to density over the soft pad, as a function of the inverted ratio of diffusivities (symbols). Points with same ν and ds differ by plate acceleration (not measured). Color shows the diffusivity over the hard disk. Vertical error bars show the standard deviations over at least 6 movies. Horizon- tal error bars indicate the difference between dispersion and...

FIG. 3. Non-equilibrium steady state with a particle current in a tilted corral. a: Relation between diffusivity and in-plane root mean square velocity. Dots show average over all runs. b: Finite-elements simulation of the steady-state Smoluchowski equation (7) for a small temperature variation (β0fR = 0.5 and ∆β/β0 = 1). Contours: ratio of the grain density ρ to the density of the base state, ρ0. White streamlines show the grain current. c: Normalized momentum as a function of normalized tilt for all experimental runs, distributed into 20 bins.
